# T02: Conflict Resolution - Name Spelling Variants

## Input

> My ancestor's name appears differently in every record:
>
> - "Dotia" (marriage certificate)
> - "Dotea" (marriage register)
> - "Theodosia" (1900 Census)
> - "Thedocia" (compiled family ahnentafel)
> - "THEODOCIA" (headstone, clearly carved)
>
> Which spelling should I use? And how do I decide?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Evaluate each spelling assertion by provenance, informant knowledge,
  purpose, timing, independence, and context, rather than by votes or a fixed
  source hierarchy
- [ ] Ask who commissioned the headstone, who supplied the inscription, and
  when it was made; do not assume family authorization from the stone alone
- [ ] Treat the clerk, census, and compiled entries as evidence to assess for
  the particular spelling, without presuming that any is automatically weaker
- [ ] Explain that the supplied facts do not establish a single authoritative
  spelling; preserve all variants with source context
- [ ] Recommend a provisional display form only if the researcher can document
  a reason for it, and otherwise defer the choice

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT pick a spelling by frequency or majority vote
- [ ] Must NOT treat the compiled ahnentafel as automatically authoritative
- [ ] Must NOT use "primary source" or "secondary source" to classify sources
- [ ] Must NOT ignore variant spellings; all should be documented

## Ground Truth

The record descriptions alone do not establish who knew or supplied the
preferred spelling. A headstone may be highly persuasive if its commissioner,
wording source, and timing are documented, but it does not automatically defeat
the marriage, census, or compiled forms. Preserve every variant, investigate
the headstone's commissioning and the informants or record purposes for the
other entries, and defer a standard display form unless that fact-specific
analysis supports one.
